coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous measurement of ochratoxin A and aflatoxin B1 using a duplexed-electrochemical aptasensor based on carbon nanodots decorated with gold nanoparticles and two redox probes hemin@HKUST-1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1
coredata.dc:creator: Jahangiri–Dehaghani, Fahime
Zare, Hamid R.
Shekari, Zahra
coredata.dc:description: An electrochemical aptasensor was developed for the simultaneous determination of ochratoxin A (OTA) and aflatoxin B1 (AFB1). Two compounds hemin@HKUST-1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1 were synthesized by encapsulating hemin and ferrocene inside the MOF made by copper nodes and trimesic acid, known as HKUST-1 MOF, and then bind to complementary DNA sequences of OTA aptamer (cDNA1) and AFB1 aptamer (cDNA2), respectively. Then, the hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2 bioconjugates were deposited on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with carbon nanodots decorated with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs-CNDs). In the absence of the two mycotoxins, the current response related to the electroactive labels of hemin and ferrocene in the discussed bioconjugates have been measured at two separate potentials simultaneously by the differential pulse voltammetry technique (DPV). Using the described aptasensor, it is possible to measure both OTA and AFB1 mycotoxins in the linear concentration range of 1.0 × 10−2 to 100.0 ng mL−1. Also, the detection limits of OTA and AFB1 were 4.3 × 10−3 ng mL−1 and 5.2 × 10−3 ng mL−1, respectively. Finally, the ability of the proposed duplex aptasensor for the simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1 in a corn flour sample was investigated and completely satisfactory results were achieved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hemin@HKUST-1
Ferrocene@HKUST-1
AuNPs−CNDs nanocomposite
Ochratoxin A
Aflatoxin B1
Electrochemical aptasensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst misctext primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of bioconjugates of hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2
Preparation of the duplexed aptasensor for the simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1
Characterization of AuNPs-CNDs nanocomposite, HKUST-1, hemin@HKUST-1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1
Electrochemical follow-up of the duplexed aptasensor
Analytical performance of the duplexed aptasensor for simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1
Repeatability, stability, reproducibility and selectivity of the duplexed aptasensor
Simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1 in a corn flour sample by the duplexed aptasensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) the distribution histogram of CNDs. (C) FE–SEM image of HKUST-1.

(A) Nyquist plots and (B) cyclic voltammograms of BGCE, AuNPs-CNDs/GCE, Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE, Bioconj/Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE, AFB1/Bioconj/Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE, and OTA/AFB1/Bioconj/Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 M KCl. The concentration of OTA and AFB1 was 10.0 ng mL−1. A frequency range of 10−1 to 105 Hz and the potential scan rate of 25 mV s−1 were used in EIS and CV, respectively.

Optimization of the conditions for the fabrication of the duplexed aptasensor. (A) The OTA and AFB1 aptamers concentrations, (B) the immobilization time of OTA and AFB1 aptamers, (C) the incubation time of bioconjugates and (D) the incubation time of OTA and AFB1 mycotoxins. The concentration of both mycotoxins was 10.0 ng mL−1 in all experiments. Error bars correspond to the standard deviation of three replicate measurements.

(A) DPV responses of the developed aptasensor in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). Numbers 1 to 7 show voltammograms related to concentrations of 1.0 × 10−2, 1.0 × 10−1, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 50.0 and 100.0 ng mL−1 of OTA and AFB1. (B) and (C) show the calibration plots obtained from changes in the peak currents, (ΔI1=Ib1-Ia1 and ΔI2=Ib2-Ia2), of hemin (ΔI1) and ferrocene (ΔI2) encapsulated in the bioconjugates before (Ib1 and Ib2) and after (Ia1 and Ia2) incubation of OTA and AFB1 with the respective aptamers vs. the logarithm of the OTA and AFB1 concentrations. The error bars correspond to the standard deviation of three replicate measurements.

(A) The responses of an OTA constant concentration (1.0 ng mL−1) in the presence of different concentrations of AFB1 (0.1, 1.0 and 10.0 ng mL−1) and an AFB1 constant concentration (1.0 ng mL−1) in the presence of different concentrations of OTA (0.1, 1.0 and 10.0 ng mL−1). (B) Selectivity study of the developed aptasensor for simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1 in the presence of AFM1. The concentrations of all three mycotoxins, ochratoxin A (OTA), aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) and aflatoxin M1 (AFM1), are 10.0 ng mL−1. The error bars correspond to the standard deviation of three replicate measurements.
The recovery results of using the developed aptasensor for the simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1 in a corn flour sample (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Simultaneous measurement of ochratoxin A and aflatoxin B1 using a duplexed-electrochemical aptasensor based on carbon nanodots decorated with gold nanoparticles and two redox probes hemin@HKUST-1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Yazd University, Yazd, 89195–741, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Yazd University, Yazd, 89195-741, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An electrochemical aptasensor was developed for the simultaneous determination of ochratoxin A (OTA) and aflatoxin B1 (AFB1). Two compounds hemin@HKUST-1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1 were synthesized by encapsulating hemin and ferrocene inside the MOF made by copper nodes and trimesic acid, known as HKUST-1 MOF, and then bind to complementary DNA sequences of OTA aptamer (cDNA1) and AFB1 aptamer (cDNA2), respectively. Then, the hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2 bioconjugates were deposited on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with carbon nanodots decorated with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs-CNDs). In the absence of the two mycotoxins, the current response related to the electroactive labels of hemin and ferrocene in the discussed bioconjugates have been measured at two separate potentials simultaneously by the differential pulse voltammetry technique (DPV). Using the described aptasensor, it is possible to measure both OTA and AFB1 mycotoxins in the linear concentration range of 1.0 × 10−2 to 100.0 ng mL−1. Also, the detection limits of OTA and AFB1 were 4.3 × 10−3 ng mL−1 and 5.2 × 10−3 ng mL−1, respectively. Finally, the ability of the proposed duplex aptasensor for the simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1 in a corn flour sample was investigated and completely satisfactory results were achieved.

Schematic illustrated the different steps of fabricating the duplexed electrochemical aptasensor based on the AuNPs-CNDs nanocomposite, hemin@HKUST-1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1 redox probes for simultaneous measurement of ochratoxin A (OTA) and aflatoxin B1 (AFB1).
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mycotoxins are secondary metabolites produced by fungi and can cause serious problems to human health through contamination of various foods and animal feeds [1,2]. They affect a wide range of agricultural products and derived products from them [3,4]. Various flours, as one of the food items, are susceptible to contamination with mycotoxins if placed in inappropriate temperature and humidity conditions. Corn flour is a very valuable and useful food for bode health. This flour is contaminated with mycotoxins in tropical regions with increasing temperature and humidity [5,6]. Aflatoxins and ochratoxins are two important classes of mycotoxins and are the source of many foodborne diseases. Aflatoxins are divided into different types such as AFG1, AFG2, AFB1, AFB2, AFM1 and AFM2. Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) has been classified as a group I carcinogen in humans by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) [7]. Furthermore, it has been reported that exposure to AFB1 can cause severe diseases such as liver cirrhosis, necrosis, and carcinoma in humans and animals [8]. Ochratoxin A (OTA) is another type of highly toxic mycotoxin that IARC has classified as a potential carcinogen [9,10]. OTA mainly affects the liver and kidney in humans and animals and can cause nephrotoxic, carcinogenic, hepatotoxic, neurotoxic, mutagenic, teratogenic, and immunotoxic effects in most mammalian species [11]. Various analytical techniques have been used to identify and quantify mycotoxins [12–14]. The use of aptamer-based electrochemical biosensors for mycotoxins detection has been reported [15,16]. This use is mostly due to low cost, simple preparation, wide linear range, ease of instrument miniaturization and high sensitivity. Aptamers are single-strand DNA or RNA oligonucleotides that exhibit specific binding affinity for a wide variety of targets [17]. The performance of biosensors is improved by using nanomaterials in their design. Among the large family of nanomaterials, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have excellent properties such as high stability, good conductivity, easy preparation, low cost, excellent biocompatibility, and convenient surface functionalization [18,19]. Also, carbon nanodots (CNDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm [20] and have characteristics such as low cost, ease of preparation, and low toxicity [21]. It has been reported that metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are appropriate substrates for the fabrication of biosensors [22–25]. These materials have interesting properties such as high surface area, tunable pore size, uniformly structured pores, ultrahigh porosity, high thermal stability, and adjustable chemical functionality [26]. In addition, the loading of some electroactive materials in the structure of MOFs makes them suitable labels for use in electrochemical biosensors.

In the present study, the electrode surface was modified by AuNPs-CNDs nanocomposite. A MOF named HKUST-1 (Hong Kong University of Science and Technology-1) has also been synthesized by copper nodes and trimesic acid. Then, hemin and ferrocene (as electroactive labels) are encapsulated inside HKUST-1, and in the next step, hemin@HKUST-1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1 bind to OTA aptamer (cDNA1) and AFB1 aptamer (cDNA2), respectively. Finally, the bioconjugates of hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2 have been used for simultaneous measurement of two mycotoxins OTA and AFB1. In this research, by modification the electrode surface by AuNPs-CNDs, the effective surface of the electrode and the sensitivity of the measurement increases. In addition, the modification of the electrode surface by MOF provides a suitable substrate with appropriate functional groups for the immobilization of biological elements. The possibility of determining very low concentration of toxins in real samples with high sensitivity and accuracy is another advantage of this electrochemical aptasensor. It is possible that other mycotoxins can also be measured using this technique and choosing appropriate aptamers. Although simultaneous measurement of two mycotoxins has been reported previously, to our knowledge, this is the first time that simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1 has been performed using hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2.

Details of materials, instruments and methods, as well as the synthesis of gold nanoparticles decorated on carbon nanodots (AuNPs-CNDs) nanocomposite, the preparation of HKUST-1, hemin @HKUST-1 and ferrocene @HKUST-1 and preparation of real sample used in this study, are given in the Support Information, sections S1-S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of bioconjugates of hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare bioconjugate of hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1, 10.0 μL of hemin@HKUST-1, 10.0 μL of a solution of EDC/NHS (4:1 ratio), and 40.0 μL of phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 0.1 M) were mixed together. This solution was stirred for about 45 min at room temperature. Then, 5.0 μL of a solution of cDNA1 (5.0 μM) was added to the mixture and stirred again for 4 h at room temperature. Free cDNA1 was removed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm and washed with 10.0 mM Tris-HCl to obtain the hemin@HKUST–1 linked to cDNA1 bioconjugate (hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1). Afterward, the bioconjugate (hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1) was dispersed in the phosphate buffer (0.05 M, pH 7.0) containing 0.3 M NaCl. Ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2 was synthesized in a similar method to hemin@HKUST–1/cDNA1, except that 10.0 μL ferrocene@HKUST-1 was replaced by 10.0 μL hemin@HKUST-1 and 5.0 μL of cDNA2 was displaced by 5.0 μL of cDNA1 (Scheme S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the duplexed aptasensor for the simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1
ce:section.ce:para: First, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was carefully polished with alumina slurry and then washed. After that, a thin layer of AuNP-CND nanocomposite (1.0 mg mL−1) was placed on the GCE surface and the solvent was evaporated at room temperature. Then, AuNPs-CNDs/GCE was immersed in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 1.0 mM of 3–mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) and 10 potential cycles between −0.2 and 0.8 V at 0.1 V s−1 were performed. Next, to activate the –COOH groups of CND, the modified electrode (AuNPs-CNDs/GCE) was immersed in phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.0) containing EDC/NHS (ratio 4:1) for 20 min. To immobilize the OTA aptamer and AFB1 aptamer, 5.0 μl of a mixture of 5.0 μM OTA aptamer and 7.0 μM AFB1 aptamer (in a ratio 1:1) was placed on the electrode surface and kept in a high-humidity container for 90 min. A humid environment was used to prevent evaporation so that the aptamer could easily bind to the electrode surface. Then, the modified electrode (Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE) was rinsed with the phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4) in order to remove any unbounded aptamers. After washing, 5.0 μL of BSA (1%, w/w) was placed on the electrode surface and incubated for another 30 min to block any possible active sites. After that, in order to perform the hybridization process, 5.0 μl of a mixture of the two bioconjugates of hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2, in a 1:1 ratio, was placed on the electrode surface and incubated for 90 min to prepare Bioconj/Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE. Immobilized aptamers were first hybridized with their complementary oligonucleotides. Therefore, by the hybridization process, cDNA1 linked to hemin@HKUST-1 binds to the OTA aptamer while cDNA2 linked to ferrocene@HKUST-1 binds to the AFB1 aptamer. After that, 5.0 μL of a mixture of OTA and AFB1 with different concentrations was added to the electrode surface and then incubated for 90 min to prepare OTA/AFB1/Bioconj/Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE. Finally, differential pulse voltammograms of the biosensor were recorded for the simultaneous determination of OTA and AFB1 mycotoxins. Scheme S2 shows the preparation method of the discussed dual aptasensor for simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1. In the presence of two mycotoxins, the hybridized bioconjugates with the immobilized aptamers were released and OTA and AFB1 interacted with their corresponding aptamers. Release of the bioconjugates decreases the current response of the electroactive markers (hemin and ferrocene). The difference in the peak currents of hemin and ferrocene before and after incubation with OTA and AFB1 was considered as an analytical signal for the simultaneous measurement of the two mycotoxins. In the absence of the two mycotoxins, the current response associated with the electroactive markers of hemin and ferrocene in the bioconjugates was measured simultaneously at two separate potentials using the differential pulse voltammetry technique. The peak potential and peak current of each marker (ferrocene and hemin) represent the nature and concentration of each mycotoxin (AFB1 and OTA), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of AuNPs-CNDs nanocomposite, HKUST-1, hemin@HKUST-1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image of CNDs is presented in Fig. 1 A. As shown in this figure, CNDs have spherical structures. Fig. 1B displays a distribution histogram of CNDs plotted on the basis of the TEM image data. According to this distribution histogram, the average size of CNDs is 7.5 ± 3.2 nm (Fig. 1B). The FE–SEM image of HKUST-1 is shown in Fig. 1C. As it can be seen, HKUST-1 has an octahedron morphology. FT-IR spectroscopy has also been used to characterize CNDs (Fig. S1A). Briefly, the broad absorption bands at 3100 to 3300 cm−1 are attributed to the O–H and N–H stretching vibration. The peaks at 1237 cm−1, 1176 cm−1, 1434 cm−1, 1324 cm−1, 2968 cm−1, and 1702 cm−1 represent the presence of C–S, C–O, CN, C–N, C–H, and CO groups, respectively. Fig. S1B shows the FT-IR spectra of HKUST-1 (curve a), ferrocene@HKUST-1 (curve b) and hemin@HKUST-1 (curve c), respectively. The presence of the absorption bands at 1553 cm−1 on the spectrum of HKUST-1 (curve a) is attributed to the CO stretching vibration, and the adsorption bands near 600 and 700 cm−1 are related to the C–H stretching of the aromatic rings. The peak around 1106 is due to the stretching vibration of C–O. The adsorption stretching peak around 3220 cm−1 corresponds to O–H group. In the FT-IR spectrum of ferrocene@HKUST-1 (curve b), a band in the 1665 cm−1 region is related to the stretching vibration of CC, and the adsorption bands near 740 and 884 cm−1 are attributed to the C–H stretching of the aromatic rings in the ferrocene molecule. Also, the new bands at 1634 cm−1 and 1368 cm−1 in hemin@HKUST-1 are related to the stretching of the CN bond and the C–N bond of the hemin molecule (curve c). Fig. S2 shows the UV–Vis absorption of the CNDs. An absorption peak around 352 nm is observed, which can be assigned to n-π* transition of the CO bond and the trapping of the excited-state energy of the surface state. The inset of Fig. S2 shows a solution of CNDs under UV light. In addition, the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) images of HKUST-1, ferrocene@HKUST-1, and hemin@HKUST-1 are displayed in Fig. S3. The EDS image of HKUST-1 (Fig. S3A) shows the peaks corresponding to carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen, and copper elements. The presence of iron in the EDS images of ferrocene@HKUST-1 (Fig. S3B) and hemin@HKUST-1 (Fig. S3C) proves that the hemin and ferrocene are loaded in HKUST-1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical follow-up of the duplexed aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The step-by-step fabrication process of the duplexed aptasensor was investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques. Fig. 2 shows the Nyquist plots (Fig. 2A) and the cyclic voltammograms (Fig. 2B) of bare GCE (BGCE), AuNPs-CNDs/GCE, Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE, Bioconj/Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE, AFB1/Bioconj/Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE and OTA/AFB1/Bioconj/Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.1 M KCl. As shown in Fig. 2A, it can be seen that the BGCE exhibits a very small semicircular domain. After modification of GCE with AuNP-CND nanocomposite, the diameter of the semicircle decreases. This can be attributed to the good conductivity of the AuNPs-CNDs nanocomposite. After immobilizing the OTA and AFB1 aptamers on the AuNPs-CNDs/GCE surface, Rct value increases. This can be attributed to the negatively charged phosphate backbone, which hinders the electron transfer process of the Fe(CN)6]3–/4– redox probe. By placing two bioconjugates (hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2) on the electrode surface and performing the hybridization process, Rct increases further. This increment can be related to the repulsion between the redox probe and negatively charged phosphate skeletons of DNA, as well as the blocking of the electrode surface. By incubating AFB1 and subsequently OTA on the modified electrode surface, Rct value increased. The increase in Rct in the presence of AFB1 and OTA can be due to the binding of AFB1 and OTA to the aptamer immobilized on the electrode surface and the formation of AFB1 aptamer/AFB1 and OTA aptamer/OTA complexes. The formation of these complexes causes more blocking of the surface and thus increasing the resistance. Fig. 2B shows that with the cyclic voltammetry method, it is possible to follow the steps of making the aptasensor and obtain similar results.

To achieve the best analytical performance, the optimal conditions related to the concentration of OTA and AFB1 aptamers, the time required for the immobilization of OTA and AFB1 aptamers, the time required for the hybridization of both bioconjugates (hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2) with the respective aptamers and the incubation time of OTA and AFB1 were investigated. In this regard, the differential pulse voltammograms of each aptasensor were recorded before and after incubation of OTA or AFB1 with respective aptamers in the phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4). The difference in peak current responses (ΔI1 = Ib1 - Ia1 and ΔI2 = Ib2 - Ia2) of hemin and ferrocene encapsulated in the bioconjugates before (Ib1 and Ib2) and after (Ia1 and Ia2) incubation of OTA and AFB1 with the respective aptamers, respectively, were considered as the analytical signal for OTA and AFB1 measurement.

In order to optimize the concentrations of OTA and AFB1 aptamers, 5.0 μL of different concentrations (i.e. 2.0, 5.0, 7.0 and 10.0 μM) of the OTA or AFB1 aptamer were separately placed on the AuNPs-CNDs/GCE surface for 90 min. Then, 5.0 μL of each bioconjugate (hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 or ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2) was incubated on the surface of the modified electrodes for 60 min. In the next step, OTA (10.0 ng mL−1) or AFB1 (10.0 ng mL−1) was incubated on the surface of the modified electrodes for 90 min. Finally, the differential pulse voltammograms were recorded in the phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4). Fig. 3 A shows that the ΔI1 and ΔI2 increased with increasing OTA aptamer concentration to 5.0 μM and AFB1 aptamer concentration to 7.0 μM and then decreased slightly for both aptamers. Therefore, the concentrations of 5.0 μM and 7.0 μM were selected as the optimal values for OTA and AFB1 aptamers, respectively. The immobilization time of OTA and AFB1 aptamers on the modified electrode surfaces was also optimized. For this purpose, 5.0 μL of OTA aptamer (5.0 μM) and AFB1 aptamer (7.0 μM) were individually placed on the surface of AuNP-CND modified electrodes. In the next step, each electrode was kept in a humid chamber for various periods (60, 90, 120 and 180 min). Then hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2 bioconjugates were incubated on the surface of the OTA and AFB1 aptasensors, respectively for 60 min. After that, OTA (10.0 ng mL−1) and AFB1 (10.0 ng mL−1) were placed on the surface of the modified electrodes for 90 min. The results are presented in Fig. 3B. As shown in this figure, as the incubation time of both OTA and AFB1 aptamers increases from 60 to 90 min, the values of ΔI1 and ΔI2 increase and decrease after 90 min. Therefore, 90 min was considered as the optimal times for immobilization of OTA and AFB1 aptamers on the modified electrodes surface. To determine the optimal time for hybridization of cDNA1 present in hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 bioconjugate with OTA aptamer and cDNA2 present in ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2 bioconjugate with AFB1 aptamer, OTA aptamer (5.0 μL, 5.0 μM) and AFB1 aptamer (5.0 μL, 7.0 μM) were individually placed on the surface of the modified electrodes, and each electrode was kept in a moist chamber for 90 min. After that, 5.0 μL of bioconjugates (hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2) were placed on the surface of the modified electrodes and incubated for different times (30, 60, 90–120 min). Afterward, OTA (5.0 μL, 10.0 ng mL−1) and AFB1 (5.0 μL, 10.0 ng mL−1) were incubated on the surface of the aptasensors for 90 min. Then, the voltammograms of each aptasensor were recorded in the phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4). Fig. 3C illustrates that the maximum responses were achieved at 90 and 60 min hybridization time for cDNA1 and cDNA2, respectively. Therefore, the optimal times for hybridization of cDNA1 and cDNA2 with OTA aptamer and AFB1 aptamer were selected as 90 min. The effect of OTA and AFB1 incubation times on the modified electrode surface was also studied. For this purpose, OTA aptamer (5.0 μM) and AFB1 aptamer (7.0 μM) were immobilized on the surface of the modified electrodes for 90 min. Bioconjugates (hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2) were also incubated on the respective aptasensors under optimal conditions for 60 min. Then, 5.0 μL of each mycotoxin was placed on the surface of the corresponding aptasensors, and they were incubated for different periods of time (30, 90, 120 and 180 min). Finally, the differential pulse voltammograms of both aptasensors were taken in phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4) and plots of the values of ΔI1 and ΔI2 were drawn versus the incubation times of AFB1 and OTA. As it can be seen in Fig. 3D, the maximum values for ΔI1 and ΔI2 are related to the incubation time of 90 min for OTA and AFB1. Thus, 90 min was considered as the optimal time for incubation of OTA and AFB1.

The optimal conditions obtained above were also used to make an electrochemical aptasensor used for the simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB mycotoxins. For this purpose, a mixture of 5.0 μM OTA aptamer and 7.0 μM AFB1 aptamer (1:1 ratio) was placed on the surface of AuNPs-CNDs/GCE and incubated for 90 min. After that, the modified electrode (Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE) was incubated with a solution containing a mixture of bioconjugates (hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene @ HKUST-1/cDNA2, 1:1) for 90 min. Next, 5.0 μl of a mixture of both mycotoxins (10 ng mL−1 in a ratio of 1:1) was placed on the surface of Bioconj/Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE and incubated for 90 min (OTA/AFB1/Bioconj/Apts/AuNPs-CNDs/GCE). Finally, the differential pulse voltammograms of the final modified electrode was recorded in phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4). Then, the current responses related to hemin and ferrocene, which appears in two separate potential, were measured and used for simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1 mycotoxins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the duplexed aptasensor for simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of the fabricated duplexed aptasensor was investigated by recording differential pulse voltammogram of the develop aptasensor to measure different concentrations of OTA and AFB1. Fig. 4 A shows DPVs of the duplexed aptasensor for 1.0 × 10−2–100.0 ng mL−1 of OTA (I) and AFB1 (II) in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). It should be noted that the peak currents I and II related to the oxidation of hemin and ferrocene, respectively. Therefore, the peaks currents of hemin and ferrocene in bioconjugates (hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2) should decrease with increasing concentration the OTA and AFB1 mycotoxins, respectively. The difference of the responses of each current in the absence and presence of the respective mycotoxin (ΔI1=Ib1-Ia1 and ΔI2=Ib2-Ia2) can be considered as the analytical signals to measure OTA and AFB1. The calibration plots obtained from the voltammogram data of Fig. 4A are shown in Fig. 4B and C. As shown in these figures, the ΔI1 and ΔI2 values increased with increasing OTA and AFB1 concentrations. In both calibration plots, in the concentrations range of 1.0 × 10−2–100.0 ng mL−1, there is a linear relationship between the values of ΔI1 and ΔI2 and the logarithm of the OTA and AFB1 concentrations. The detection limits of OTA and AFB1 were calculated according to the equation yl. o.d. = ybl + 3sbl. In this equation, sbl and ybl represent the standard deviations and mean of the blank response [24]. Using this equation and calibration plots, the detection limits of OTA and AFB1 were obtained 4.3 × 10−3 ng mL−1 and 5.2 × 10−3 ng mL−1, respectively. Some of the analytical parameters for the simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1 by means of the proposed aptasensor and other aptasensors are compared in Table S1. The results of this comparison and the data in Table S1 indicate that the linear concentration range and detection limit of OTA and AFB1 obtained using the proposed duplexed aptasensor are comparable to or better than those reported in the literature. For example, Xiao et al. reported a linear concentration range of 5.0 × 10−2 -5.0 ng mL−1 for OTA and AFB1 and detection limits of 2.6 × 10−2 ng mL−1 and 2.1 × 10−2 ng mL−1 for OTA and AFB1 [27]. Wang et al. using the HPLC–MS/MS method obtained the detection limits of 1.2 × 10−2 ng mL−1 and 2.4 × 10−2 ng mL−1 for OTA and AFB1. Also, a linear concentration range between 5.0 × 10−3 and 1.5 ng mL−1 has been reported for the quantitative measurement of both mycotoxins [28]. Irakliand et al. reported detection limits of 3.6 × 10−1 ng mL−1 and 4.0 × 10−1 ng mL−1 for OTA and AFB1 using HPLC-DAD-FLD method. They also reported a linear concentration range between 5.0 × 10−1 to 25.0 ng mL−1 for the simultaneous determination of both mycotoxins [29]. In addition, the proposed aptasensor has some advantages over the published papers by our group [15,16,19,22]. In this work, despite the simultaneous measurement of two aflatoxins, the detection limit of aflatoxins measurement has been improved compared to some previous works [16,19]. Also, the time to make the electrochemical aptasensor has been shortened [15,16,19,22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability, stability, reproducibility and selectivity of the duplexed aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the repeatability of the aptasensor, the responses of the duplexed aptasensor were recorded after incubation with 5.0 ng mL−1 OTA and 5.0 ng mL−1 AFB1 in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). The relative standard deviation (RSD) for repeated measurements (n = 4) was obtained 3.4% and 5.0% for OTA and AFB1, respectively. The stability of the aptasensor was also investigated. For this purpose, the designed aptasensor was kept for seven days at 4 °C, and during this storage period, it was used to measure 5.0 ng mL−1 OTA and 5.0 ng mL−1 AFB1. The results indicated that the response of the aptasensor for 5.0 ng mL−1 OTA and 5.0 ng mL−1AFB1 reaches about 95.6% and 96.7% after two days and about 93.1% and 94.3% after seven days compared to the initial responses. In order to evaluate the reproducibility of the designed aptasensor, a mixture of two mycotoxins (5.0 ng mL−1) was prepared and incubated on the surface of four independently prepared aptasensors. Then, the differential pulse voltammograms of all four aptasensor were recorded and the values ΔI1 and ΔI2 were obtained for each them. The RSD values of the current response were obtained 4.7% for OTA and 6.5% for AFB1. These results show that the discussed aptasensor has satisfactory repeatability, stability, and reproducibility.

To demonstrate the ability of the sensor to measure one of two mycotoxins in the presence of different concentrations of the other, the response of the proposed aptasensor to the measurement of one mycotoxin at constant concentration of the other mycotoxin was evaluated. For this purpose, the differential pulse voltammograms of the proposed aptasensor were recorded for a constant concentration of OTA (1.0 ng mL−1) and different concentrations of AFB1 (0.1, 1.0 and 10.0 ng mL−1), as well as a constant concentration of AFB1 (1.0 ng mL−1) and different concentrations of OTA (0.1, 1.0 and 10.0 ng mL−1), and the values of ΔI1 and ΔI2 were calculated. The results shown in Fig. 5 A confirm that the responses of OTA and AFB1 at the surface of the proposed aptasensor are relatively independent of each other.

Moreover, to investigate the selectivity of the duplexed aptasensor, a solution of 10.0 ng mL−1 OTA and 10.0 ng mL−1 AFB1 was mixed with 10.0 ng mL−1 AFM1 and incubated on the surface of the proposed aptasensor. After recording the differential pulse voltammograms, the values of ΔI1 and ΔI2 were calculated for OTA and AFB1. The results are shown in Fig. 5B. As it can be observed, the value of ΔI1 did not change in the presence of AFM1 but the value of ΔI2 increased. Therefore, AFM1 does not interfere with OTA measurement, but interferes with the aptamer response for AFB1 the measurement. The reason for this can be attributed to the similar structure of AFM1 to that of AFB1. These results indicate that OTA can be measured simultaneously with AFB1 or AFM1 using the designed aptasensor. However, this aptasensor is unable to distinguish the responses of AFB1 and AFM1 simultaneously. In addition, the interference of some ions in the simultaneous measurement of the two mycotoxins under optimal conditions was investigated. For this purpose, 10.0 ng mL−1 of a mixture of two mycotoxins (in a ratio 1:1) was mixed with compounds containing 1.5 mg magnesium ion, 3.0 mg potassium ion, 0.5 mg sodium ion, 0.1 mg calcium ion, 0.01 mg manganese ion, 0.02 mg zinc ion, 0.03 mg iron (II) ion and 0.01 mg copper ion and then placed on the modified electrode surface. The corresponding differential pulse voltammograms were recorded and the values of ΔI1 and ΔI2 were calculated. The results showed that the presence of these ions does not interfere with the simultaneous measurement of two mycotoxins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1 in a corn flour sample by the duplexed aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the results of using the duplexed aptasensor for the simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1 in corn flour samples are reported. As described in section S4, first mycotoxins-contaminated corn flour samples were prepared. The prepared solutions were incubated on the duplexed aptasensor surface and the electrochemical responses were recorded. The results of this measurement are reported in Table 1 . The data in Table 1 indicate that the recovery of OTA and AFB1 in the corn flour sample is in the range of 96.0–110.0% with RSD less than 10%. In addition, the results in this table demonstrate that the proposed duplexed aptasensor has a good ability to simultaneously measure OTA and AFB1 in the corn flour sample.

The construction and performance of a duplexed electrochemical aptasensor suitable for the simultaneous measurement of two mycotoxins OTA and AFB1 are reported. AuNPs-CNDs modified GCE was used as the sensing substrate. Hemin@HKUST-1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1 bound to cDNA1 and cDNA2 (hemin@HKUST-1/cDNA1 and ferrocene@HKUST-1/cDNA2) were used as two electroactive bioconjugates for simultaneous measurement of OTA and AFB1, respectively. The simultaneous measurement of these two mycotoxins, compared to the measurement of each species, shortens the time and reduces the cost of the measurement. Also, very low concentration of toxins can be determined with this aptasensor. In addition, two mycotoxins OTA and AFB1 were simultaneously measured with the proposed duplexed aptasensor in the corn flour sample and satisfactory results were obtained. However, each aptamer-based electrochemical biosensor can only be used in a one experiment and is therefore disposable. This study demonstrates a general methodology for developing sensitive and selective aptasensors for other mycotoxins by selecting appropriate aptamers.

Fahime Jahangiri–Dehaghani: Conceptualization, Writing- Original draft preparation, Investigation, Data curation, Software. Hamid R. Zare: Supervision, Conceptualization. Reviewing and Editing, Validation. Zahra Shekari: Conceptualization, Reviewing and Editing, Investigation, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.124947.
